questions.text: I can usually identify what specific emotion I am feeling in the moment.
I notice shifts in my emotional state throughout the day.
I have difficulty naming what I'm feeling beyond 'good' or 'bad'.
When I feel a strong emotion, I can choose how to respond rather than reacting automatically.
I use specific strategies to manage difficult emotions.
I often lose my temper before I realize what's happening.
I can sense how others are feeling even when they don't say anything.
I find myself absorbing the emotions of people around me.
I adjust my communication style based on others' emotional states.
I know which situations or people tend to trigger strong emotional reactions in me.
I pay attention to how my words affect others during conversations.
I notice when I'm dominating a conversation or not giving others space to speak.
I listen to understand, not just to respond.
I can identify when someone is setting a boundary, even if they don't say it directly.
I have clear personal boundaries that I communicate to others.
I notice power dynamics in group settings.
I'm aware of how my presence changes the energy of a group.
I consider the long-term impact of my actions on relationships.
I notice patterns in how my relationships with others evolve over time.
I struggle to see how my behavior affects other people.
I regularly reflect on my own thinking patterns and biases.
I can describe the reasoning process I use to make decisions.
I question my assumptions when I notice I might be wrong.
I can recognize when my judgment is being influenced by a cognitive bias.
I actively look for evidence that contradicts my existing beliefs.
I know what strategies help me learn most effectively.
I notice when I don't understand something and seek clarification.
I can hold multiple perspectives on an issue simultaneously.
I consider how someone with a very different life experience might see a situation.
I find it hard to understand why people hold views that are very different from mine.
When someone directs negativity at me, I can process it without passing it on to others.
I break cycles of negativity rather than continuing them.
I can sit with someone's pain without needing to fix it or pass it along.
When I experience something good, I naturally share it with others.
Others would say I uplift the people around me.
I notice when I'm amplifying a negative situation rather than calming it.
I sometimes make conflicts worse without meaning to.
I tend to consume positive experiences privately rather than sharing them outward.
I recognize when I'm holding onto something that would be better shared.
I notice when I'm just passing experiences through without processing them.
I am caught off guard by my emotional reactions to everyday situations.
I can anticipate how I will emotionally react in familiar situations.
I can distinguish between similar emotions like frustration and disappointment.
I frequently interrupt others before they finish speaking.
I find it difficult to say no even when I want to.
I can sense when a group is losing energy or engagement.
I rarely consider that my first impression of someone might be wrong.
I adjust my approach when I realize my current method is not working.
I serve as a bridge between people who have trouble communicating directly.
I keep good news to myself rather than celebrating with others.
I notice physical sensations in my body when I experience strong emotions.
I can tell when my body needs rest before I become exhausted.
I ignore my body's signals until they become impossible to overlook.
I am aware of my posture throughout the day.
I notice changes in my breathing when I am under stress.
I know where in my body I tend to hold tension.
I notice muscle tightness building up before it becomes pain.
I am unaware of physical tension in my body until someone points it out.
I take deliberate steps to release physical tension when I notice it.
I can connect specific physical tension patterns to recurring stressors in my life.
I can sense my energy levels rising and falling throughout the day.
I schedule demanding tasks for times when my energy is highest.
I push through fatigue without noticing how depleted I am.
I know which activities drain my energy and which replenish it.
I notice the connection between my physical energy and my emotional state.
I notice details about my physical surroundings that others often miss.
I am attuned to sounds, lighting, and temperature in my environment.
I walk into a room without noticing what has changed since my last visit.
I quickly become aware when something in my environment is out of place.
I notice the overall atmosphere of a place when I first enter it.
I understand how the arrangement of a space affects my mood and productivity.
I intentionally organize my environment to support my well-being.
I do not notice how different environments affect my mental state.
I choose where to work or relax based on how the space makes me feel.
I notice when clutter or disorder in my space is affecting my thinking.
I am sensitive to how close others stand to me in conversation.
I respect others' physical space without needing to be told.
I notice when someone seems uncomfortable with physical proximity.
I am unaware of how much physical space I take up in shared environments.
I adjust my physical positioning to make others feel comfortable.
I have a good sense of how much time has passed without checking a clock.
I notice when time seems to speed up or slow down depending on what I am doing.
I frequently lose track of time and miss deadlines or appointments.
I can accurately estimate how long a task will take me to complete.
I am aware of the pace at which I move through my day.
I trace current problems back to the decisions that led to them.
I see connections between past events and present circumstances.
I struggle to understand how my past choices have shaped my current situation.
I consider the chain of consequences before making important decisions.
I learn from cause-and-effect patterns I have observed in my own life.
I think about how my current actions will affect my life months or years from now.
I set long-term goals and work toward them consistently.
I live mostly in the present without considering future implications.
I anticipate potential obstacles and plan for them in advance.
I balance short-term desires with long-term well-being when making choices.
I notice my heartbeat changing in response to emotional situations.
I can tell the difference between hunger and anxiety in my stomach.
I am disconnected from the internal sensations of my body.
I notice subtle changes in my body temperature throughout the day.
I pay attention to how different foods make my body feel.
I recognize the early physical signs of stress before it escalates.
I notice when my body enters fight-or-flight mode.
Stress builds up in my body without me realizing it.
I use my body's stress signals as cues to take action.
I can calm my body's stress response through deliberate techniques.
I trust my gut instincts when making important decisions.
I experience a physical sense of knowing that guides my choices.
I dismiss physical intuitions because I cannot explain them logically.
I can distinguish between anxiety and genuine intuition.
I notice a physical reaction in my body when something feels wrong about a situation.
I quickly pick up on the social norms of a new environment.
I notice unspoken rules that govern behavior in different settings.
I behave the same way regardless of the context I am in.
I read the mood of a room before deciding how to engage.
I recognize when a situation calls for a different approach than my default one.
I see how organizational structures shape people's behavior.
I notice how cultural norms influence what people feel comfortable expressing.
I overlook how external systems and structures affect individual outcomes.
I consider how incentive structures drive people's decisions.
I understand that people's behavior is often a response to the systems they exist within.
I adjust my behavior effectively when I move between different social environments.
I modify my approach when I realize my environment has changed.
I resist adapting to new situations because it feels inauthentic.
I thrive in new or unfamiliar environments.
I seek to understand the expectations of a new environment before acting.
I notice recurring thought patterns that influence my behavior.
I catch myself engaging in negative self-talk.
I am unaware of the habitual thoughts that drive my daily choices.
I can observe my thoughts as if watching them from the outside.
I recognize when my thinking has become rigid or repetitive.
I am aware that my understanding of the world is based on mental models that may be incomplete.
I update my mental models when I encounter new information.
I hold onto my existing framework for understanding things even when evidence suggests otherwise.
I use different frameworks to analyze problems depending on the context.
I can articulate the mental models I use to make sense of complex situations.
I identify assumptions I am making before drawing conclusions.
I question whether my conclusions are based on facts or assumptions.
I accept my initial interpretation of events without questioning it.
I ask others to challenge my assumptions when making important decisions.
I can separate what I know from what I assume.
I am fully engaged in what I am doing at any given moment.
My mind wanders to the past or future during routine activities.
I savor pleasant experiences as they happen rather than rushing to the next thing.
I notice the small details of my current experience.
I go through daily tasks on autopilot without being aware of what I am doing.
I observe my thoughts and feelings without labeling them as good or bad.
I criticize myself harshly for having certain thoughts or feelings.
I accept my emotional experiences without trying to change them immediately.
I allow difficult feelings to pass through me without resistance.
I treat myself with kindness when I make mistakes.
I can redirect my attention when I notice it has drifted.
I struggle to maintain focus on one thing for an extended period.
I choose where to direct my attention rather than letting it be pulled by distractions.
I notice when my attention has been captured by a worry or rumination.
I practice deliberately focusing my awareness on the present.
I can sense when emotional energy is flowing between people in a conversation.
I notice the transfer of emotional states between people in a group.
I am oblivious to the emotional currents moving through a social interaction.
I perceive how positive or negative energy moves through my social network.
I recognize when the emotional tone of a group is shifting.
I can tell whether I am giving or receiving emotional energy in a relationship.
I notice which direction support flows in my closest relationships.
I do not pay attention to whether exchanges with others are balanced or one-sided.
I sense when I am being drained by an interaction versus being nourished by it.
I am aware of whether I am a net giver or receiver in my key relationships.
I notice when someone else's mood has changed my own.
I recognize when I have influenced someone else's emotional state.
I am unaware of how emotions transfer between myself and others.
I can trace the origin of a feeling I am experiencing back to an interaction with someone else.
I notice when a positive interaction with one person carries over into my next interaction.
I see myself as part of an interconnected web of relationships and influences.
I notice how different people in my life are connected to each other.
I think of my relationships in isolation rather than as part of a larger system.
I understand how changes in one relationship can affect others in my network.
I recognize the role I play within my broader social system.
I consider how my actions might affect people I do not directly interact with.
I notice how a small act of kindness or unkindness can ripple outward through a community.
I rarely think about the downstream consequences of my daily interactions.
I am aware that my emotional state can spread beyond my immediate circle.
I trace how problems in one area of my life create effects in other areas.
I recognize emotional or behavioral patterns that have been passed down in my family.
I notice how my family's coping strategies have shaped my own.
I do not see connections between my behavior and the patterns of previous generations.
I am intentional about which family patterns I want to continue and which I want to change.
I consider how my choices today might affect future generations.
I find myself passing along criticism I receive to whoever is nearby.
I actively look for ways to share positive experiences with others.
I can amplify positive energy in a group when things are going well.
I take in others' negativity without transforming it, and it weighs on me.
I pass along information and emotions without adding my own interpretation.
I can calm myself down when I notice my emotions becoming overwhelming.
I notice when I have unintentionally hurt someone's feelings.
I examine my motivations before acting on an important decision.
I notice when my jaw is clenched or my shoulders are raised.
I am aware of how the time of day and season affect my mood and energy.
I sense when emotional energy in a group is blocked or stagnant.
I notice how my emotional reactions create chain reactions in my relationships.
I bring my full attention to conversations rather than planning what to say next.
I notice when I am catastrophizing or imagining worst-case scenarios.
I notice physical signs of relaxation when I transition from a stressful to a calm environment.

scenarios.narrative: Your coworker is struggling with their workload and comes to you visibly stressed. They start venting about how overwhelmed they feel and how unfair the situation is.

choices.text: Listen deeply and help them process what they're feeling, offering calm presence
Take on their stress as your own — you can't help but feel overwhelmed too
Acknowledge their frustration briefly and suggest they talk to their manager
Feel a sense of relief or satisfaction that it's their problem, not yours

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: Can you think of a specific time this happened? What did you actually do?

scenarios.narrative: You receive unexpected recognition at work for a project you led. Your team contributed significantly, but you're the one being praised publicly.

choices.text: Immediately redirect credit to the team and amplify their contributions publicly
Accept the praise and amplify it — talk about the achievement enthusiastically
Accept it graciously but don't make a big deal about it either way
Enjoy the recognition privately and hold onto that good feeling for yourself

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: How did that feel? Did you share it with anyone afterward?

scenarios.narrative: You're having a difficult day — a series of small frustrations have been building up. You come home and your partner or roommate cheerfully asks you about your day.

choices.text: Take a breath, acknowledge your bad day honestly but without dumping the frustration on them
Vent about everything that went wrong, amplifying each frustration as you retell it
Say 'fine' and keep the frustration to yourself
Snap at them — their cheerfulness feels tone-deaf given your day

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: What usually happens when you bring stress home? How does it affect the people around you?

scenarios.narrative: A friend shares a creative project they've been working on. It's clearly meaningful to them, though you can see areas where it could be improved.

choices.text: Appreciate what's working, then offer thoughtful feedback that helps them grow
Get genuinely excited about it and share it with others to amplify their work
Say it looks nice and move on to another topic
Point out the flaws — they need honest feedback, and sugarcoating doesn't help

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: How do you typically respond when someone shares something they're proud of?

scenarios.narrative: You're at a family dinner where a heated argument breaks out between two relatives. The tension is palpable and everyone at the table can feel it.

choices.text: Calmly redirect the conversation, absorbing the tension and transforming the energy at the table
Absorb the tension silently — you feel it physically but keep it to yourself
Stay neutral and wait for it to blow over — it's not your fight
Jump in and escalate — if they're going to argue, at least get to the real issue

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: Think of the last family or group conflict you witnessed. What role did you play?

scenarios.narrative: Two friends in your social group have had a falling out and are no longer speaking. Both come to you separately to complain about the other.

choices.text: Listen to both sides, then gently help each see the other's perspective to foster reconciliation
Take on the emotional burden of both friends, feeling torn and anxious about the situation
Relay messages between them without getting emotionally involved
Enjoy the drama and the fact that both friends confide in you over each other

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: When people you care about are in conflict, what role do you naturally fall into?

scenarios.narrative: You overhear a colleague being unfairly criticized by a supervisor in a meeting. The colleague looks defeated and embarrassed.

choices.text: Speak up to offer a balanced perspective, then check in privately with your colleague afterward
Feel their embarrassment deeply and carry that heaviness with you for the rest of the day
Note it happened but stay out of it — it is between them and the supervisor
Pile on with additional criticism since the supervisor opened the door

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: Have you ever witnessed someone being treated unfairly? What did you do, and what do you wish you had done?

scenarios.narrative: You receive harsh, critical feedback on a project you worked hard on. The feedback feels personal rather than constructive.

choices.text: Process the sting privately, extract any useful insights, and respond with measured professionalism
Complain loudly to coworkers about how unfair the feedback was, rallying others to your side
Shrug it off outwardly and move on without processing it
Internalize the criticism completely and let it erode your confidence

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: How do you typically handle criticism that feels unfair? What happens to that energy?

scenarios.narrative: After a long period of financial stress, you receive an unexpected windfall — a bonus, an inheritance, or a lucky break. People around you are still struggling financially.

choices.text: Share your good fortune thoughtfully, finding ways to help those around you who are still struggling
Celebrate enthusiastically and tell everyone about your luck, amplifying the good news
Quietly accept it without drawing attention to the disparity between you and others
Keep it entirely to yourself and enjoy it privately, not wanting to share

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: When something good happens to you while others are struggling, how do you navigate that tension?

scenarios.narrative: You have learned a valuable life lesson through a painful personal experience. A younger person you mentor is about to make a similar mistake.

choices.text: Share your experience vulnerably, transforming your pain into wisdom they can use
Warn them dramatically about all the terrible things that could happen, amplifying the danger
Mention briefly that you went through something similar and let them figure it out
Stay silent — they need to learn the hard way just like you did

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: When you have hard-won wisdom, how do you decide whether and how to share it?

scenarios.narrative: You are at a social gathering where you notice someone sitting alone, looking uncomfortable. Everyone else seems to be enjoying themselves in their own groups.

choices.text: Approach them warmly, draw them into conversation, and help them feel included
Feel their discomfort acutely and become anxious yourself, but stay with your own group
Notice them but assume they prefer being alone and continue with your evening
Feel grateful that you are not the one on the outside and enjoy your own social standing

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: When you see someone being excluded, what is your instinct? Do you act on it?

scenarios.narrative: You achieve a personal milestone — running a marathon, earning a degree, or completing a major creative work. People around you react with varying levels of interest.

choices.text: Use your achievement to inspire and encourage others pursuing their own goals
Share your excitement widely on social media and in person, amplifying the celebration
Mention it casually if asked but do not make it a focal point
Feel deflated when others do not share your excitement and keep the pride to yourself

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: How do you handle your own achievements? Do you tend to share or keep them private?

scenarios.narrative: A rumor is circulating at work about potential layoffs. People are anxious and coming to you for information and reassurance.

choices.text: Acknowledge the uncertainty honestly while offering calm perspective and practical next steps
Share every piece of information you have heard, adding your own worried speculations
Pass along what you know without emotional commentary and tell people to check with HR
Use the situation to position yourself favorably with management while others worry

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: When uncertainty spreads through a group, what role do you tend to play?

scenarios.narrative: You are exhausted after a long week and have been looking forward to a quiet weekend. A close friend calls in distress, needing emotional support during a crisis.

choices.text: Set aside your own fatigue, be fully present for them, and find a way to recharge afterward
Take on their crisis fully, becoming so absorbed in their pain that you forget your own needs
Listen briefly and suggest they call a helpline or another friend who might have more energy
Resent the intrusion and give minimal, irritated responses hoping they get the hint

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: How do you balance being there for others when your own resources are depleted?

scenarios.narrative: You have been practicing meditation and self-development for years and have found deep inner peace. A family member dismisses your practices as nonsense.

choices.text: Respond with genuine compassion, sharing how the practices have helped you without needing their approval
Passionately defend your practices and try to convince them of the scientific evidence
Acknowledge their perspective without engaging further and continue your practice privately
Feel hurt and keep your personal growth journey completely hidden from family going forward

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: When others dismiss something meaningful to you, how do you respond internally and externally?

scenarios.narrative: Your team at work just successfully completed a difficult project under tight deadlines. Everyone is relieved but also drained.

choices.text: Organize a celebration, acknowledge each person's contribution, and help transform exhaustion into shared pride
Send an enthusiastic company-wide email about the achievement, amplifying the success story
Mark the completion in the project tracker and move on to the next task
Take primary credit for the outcome in your performance review without mentioning the team

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: After a shared accomplishment, what do you do with that collective energy?

scenarios.narrative: A friend confides in you that they failed an important exam they had been studying for months. They are devastated and questioning their abilities.

choices.text: Acknowledge their pain, help them separate the failure from their self-worth, and support them in making a new plan
Feel devastated alongside them and share stories of your own failures, matching their despair
Tell them that plenty of successful people have failed exams and they should try again
Secretly feel relieved that you are not in their position, or think they should have studied harder

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: When someone you care about faces failure, what is your instinctive response?

scenarios.narrative: You witness a stranger being verbally harassed on public transit. Other passengers look uncomfortable but no one is intervening.

choices.text: Calmly intervene by engaging the harassed person in conversation, defusing the situation through presence
Feel paralyzed by the tension and absorb the fear and discomfort without acting
Make eye contact with the victim to show support but do not verbally engage
Confront the harasser aggressively, escalating the situation with your own anger

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: When you witness injustice in public, what do you feel in your body and what do you do?

scenarios.narrative: Your household is going through a stressful period — money is tight, and tensions are running high between family members. Small disagreements are escalating into big fights.

choices.text: Acknowledge the stress everyone is under, create space for honest conversation, and suggest practical compromises
Add your own frustrations to the mix, making the arguments louder and more intense
Withdraw to your room and wait for things to calm down on their own
Absorb everyone's stress silently until you feel physically ill from the weight of it

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: When stress escalates in your home, what pattern do you typically follow?

scenarios.narrative: You have a profound insight during a period of deep self-reflection — something that fundamentally shifts how you see yourself and the world. You feel changed by it.

choices.text: Integrate the insight into your life and share it naturally with others when the moment is right
Tell everyone immediately about your breakthrough, eager to spread the revelation
Note it intellectually and continue with your day without letting it change much
Hold the insight close as a private treasure, reluctant to dilute it by sharing

scenarios.follow_up_prompt: When you experience a moment of deep personal insight, what do you do with it?
